coredata.dc:title: State-of-charge estimation of batteries using parameterized physics-informed neural networks
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Jang, Yu Seok
Kim, Youngjin
Kim, Jonghoon
coredata.dc:description: Accurate state-of-charge (SOC) estimation is essential for battery management systems (BMS). Conventional model-based methods suffer from initialization sensitivity and parameter dependency, while data-driven methods require extensive datasets and lack physical interpretability. This paper proposes a hybrid neural network method that integrates temporal sequence learning with parameterized physics-informed neural networks (PPINN) to estimate the SOC of the public LG 18650HG2 and laboratory-tested INR 21700-33 J lithium-ion batteries. The proposed method employs a hypernetwork-based framework that generates dynamic weights by jointly encoding SOC-dependent baseline parameter sets and LSTM-based temporal predictions, enabling adaptive learning across diverse operating conditions. This combined encoding allows a latent representation of battery states to effectively control the solution network, while physical dynamics are directly incorporated into the loss function to ensure consistency. Experimental results across various temperature conditions and driving cycles show superior performance, achieving RMSE values between 0.83% and 1.59% for the full public dataset. Furthermore, the proposed method exhibits exceptional robustness to missing data, achieving an RMSE as low as 0.92% even after a 62% reduction of the public training set. Notably, the PPINN maintains a robust RMSE of less than 2.51% in laboratory-tested scenarios, even under extreme data-scarce conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: State-of-charge (SOC)
Battery management system (BMS)
Parameterized physics-informed neural networks (PPINN)
Long short-term memory (LSTM)

Integration of LSTM predictions with the PPINN
Overall framework for physics-informed SOC estimation
Multi-objective loss function for synergistic learning
Test datasets: public and laboratory tested datasets
SOC estimation at various temperatures and drive cycles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure of LSTM unit for temporal SOC estimation.
Common equivalent circuit to represent battery dynamics.
Common procedure to estimate battery parameters: (a) HPPC test for public LG 18650HG2 datasets and (b) OCV test for laboratory-tested INR 21700-33 J datasets.
Overall PPINN framework for battery SOC estimation.
Comparison of model storage requirements between the conventional and proposed approaches.
Current profile for public LG 18650HG2 battery datasets.
Experiment battery testing setup.
Comparative SOC estimation and SOC error results at room temperature: (a) LA92 driving cycle, (b) US06 driving cycle.

Brief description of the proposed and comparative methods.

row.entry:
Data-driven + Physics-based:
A framework that adaptively integrates data patterns and physical laws to ensure accurate SOC estimation

Data-driven:
A fundamental approach that estimates SOC by learning patterns solely from measured battery data

Model-based:
A conventional model-based approach relying on established battery circuit equations for state estimation

Data-driven + Physics-based:
An approach that embeds physical laws into the network, yet is typically limited to fixed parameter states

Data-driven + Model-based:
A hybrid framework that combines data-driven patterns and model-based filtering in a parallel fusion structure

Data-driven + Model-based:
A hybrid framework that sequentially applies a model-based filter and a data-driven model for error compensation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: RMSE comparison of all methods at room temperature and driving cycles.
SVD configuration parameters and SOC estimation performance at room temperature (25 °C).
RMSE comparison of all methods across multiple temperature conditions and driving cycles.
RMSE comparison with random sampling-based training data reduction across multiple temperature conditions and driving cycles.
Training dataset size under missing data conditions.
RMSE comparison under missing data conditions across multiple temperature conditions and driving cycles.
RMSE comparison for laboratory-tested datasets with varying training sample sizes.

head.ce:title: State-of-charge estimation of batteries using parameterized physics-informed neural networks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), 37673, Pohang, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical Engineering, Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), 37673, Pohang, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Chungnam National University, 34134, Daejeon, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical Engineering, Chungnam National University, 34134, Daejeon, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate state-of-charge (SOC) estimation is essential for battery management systems (BMS). Conventional model-based methods suffer from initialization sensitivity and parameter dependency, while data-driven methods require extensive datasets and lack physical interpretability. This paper proposes a hybrid neural network method that integrates temporal sequence learning with parameterized physics-informed neural networks (PPINN) to estimate the SOC of the public LG 18650HG2 and laboratory-tested INR 21700-33 J lithium-ion batteries. The proposed method employs a hypernetwork-based framework that generates dynamic weights by jointly encoding SOC-dependent baseline parameter sets and LSTM-based temporal predictions, enabling adaptive learning across diverse operating conditions. This combined encoding allows a latent representation of battery states to effectively control the solution network, while physical dynamics are directly incorporated into the loss function to ensure consistency. Experimental results across various temperature conditions and driving cycles show superior performance, achieving RMSE values between 0.83% and 1.59% for the full public dataset. Furthermore, the proposed method exhibits exceptional robustness to missing data, achieving an RMSE as low as 0.92% even after a 62% reduction of the public training set. Notably, the PPINN maintains a robust RMSE of less than 2.51% in laboratory-tested scenarios, even under extreme data-scarce conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The global transition toward sustainable transportation has fundamentally transformed the automotive industry, positioning electric vehicles (EVs) as a critical component of environmental protection and energy security strategies [1]. EV adoption has experienced unprecedented growth, driven by technological advancements and supportive policy frameworks, with the electrification of road transport representing a paradigm shift toward decarbonization [2,3]. Lithium-ion batteries have emerged as the dominant energy storage solution due to their superior characteristics, including high specific energy, exceptional efficiency, and extended operational lifetime [4,5].

Accurate state-of-charge (SOC) estimation of EV batteries is essential for improved safety by preventing overcharge and over-discharge conditions, extending battery life through proper charge management, optimizing performance via efficient energy consumption strategies, and enabling effective system control for energy management operations [6]. However, SOC estimation faces significant challenges in BMS, including nonlinear battery characteristics, aging effects, and initial state uncertainty.

Model-based methods for SOC estimation, particularly the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), offer advantages including data efficiency with low data requirements and physical insight through battery dynamics reflection and electrochemical behavior modeling [7]. However, these methods suffer from fundamental limitations, including high model dependency, initialization sensitivity, and adaptation limitations [8,9]. Despite advances in parameter identification methods including online identification and optimization algorithms [10–13], these methods still face sensitivity to experimental conditions and reduced adaptability to real-time parameter variations.

Data-driven methods, particularly recurrent neural networks (RNN) such as LSTM and gated recurrent unit (GRU), have gained significant attention as model-independent alternatives for SOC estimation [14]. These methods offer advantages including data adaptability with high learning capability from various patterns and aging effects through automatic adaptation to battery degradation [15]. However, these RNN-based methods exhibit inherent limitations including data dependency requiring large training datasets, physical insight limitations due to black-box approach constraints, and excessive dependence on data patterns resulting in poor stability under unseen conditions [16].

Hybrid methods combining model-based and data-driven methods have emerged to leverage complementary advantages of both methods for enhanced SOC estimation. [17] demonstrates improved accuracy through parallel combination of optimization algorithms with temporal pattern recognition, utilizing particle swarm optimization (PSO) to enhance LSTM learning while maintaining independent Kalman filtering for state estimation. Similarly, [18] shows enhanced performance by sequentially applying EKF for initial estimation followed by LSTM, enabling both physical constraints and data-driven adaptability. These hybrid methods aim to overcome individual limitations through parallel or sequential integration of different estimation methods. However, these methods still present fundamental drawbacks including limited physical interpretability, computational complexity from multiple algorithm integration, and parameter estimation dependency that can propagate errors.

Rather than combining separate data-driven and model-based methods, Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINN) integrate domain-specific physical laws directly into the neural network framework. While the primary implementation involves embedding governing equations directly into the loss function [19], various architectural strategies have been explored to enforce physical consistency and boundary conditions. This methodology allows PINN to simultaneously leverage observed data and underlying governing equations, providing physically consistent solutions while learning complex patterns from data [20]. For battery SOC estimation, PINN effectively infers the SOC and internal states by incorporating electrochemical dynamics into the learning process, thereby inherently reflecting the underlying physical principles [21]. This enhances physical reliability and reduces the requirement for large training datasets, positioning PINN as a compelling solution for accurate battery state estimation. However, conventional PINN exhibits significant challenges in complex, parameterized conditions. Although efforts to improve the electrochemical fidelity of PINN-based methods have shown promise [22], they often suffer from limited parameter adaptability in real-time operation, as the physical parameters are treated as fixed constants requiring separate retraining when conditions change. Specifically, the inherent PINN framework struggles with dynamic battery behavior that varies across operating temperatures and aging states [23]. This results in initialization sensitivity and poor generalization when battery parameters vary continuously as a function of SOC and operating conditions. Furthermore, although PINN has been applied to battery degradation modeling [24], its reliance on fixed physical parameters inherently limits its adaptability to multi-parameter variations under dynamic conditions.

To address these fundamental limitations, recent advances have focused on developing parameterized physics-informed neural networks (PPINN) capable of efficiently handling varying parameter conditions [25]. The core challenge involves training a single neural network to learn a family of solutions across multiple parameter configurations, thereby eliminating the need for repetitive training. To this end, hypernetwork-based approaches have emerged as a particularly effective solution, where a hypernetwork generates dynamic weights for the primary solution network based on specific parameter inputs, enabling the model to dynamically adapt its behavior to diverse operating conditions [26]. To further improve training stability and computational efficiency, this PPINN method integrates several key techniques designed to address specific implementation challenges. To overcome the slow convergence associated with complex parameterized partial differential equations (PDE), meta-learning initialization methods are employed to establish an optimal starting point, significantly accelerating the training process [27]. This optimization is complemented by structural refinements aimed at enhancing operational efficiency. In particular, low-rank approximations via singular value decomposition (SVD) are utilized to compress the network's weight matrices. This approach effectively reduces the number of trainable parameters and operational complexity [28,29]. Furthermore, to ensure robust learning across multiple objectives, self-adaptive loss balancing (SALB) mechanisms are incorporated to automatically adjust the relative importance of data fidelity and physics-based constraints, thereby eliminating the need for manual hyperparameter tuning [30].

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 describes the LSTM framework for temporal SOC estimation. Section 3 presents the proposed PPINN framework including hypernetwork design and multi-objective loss function. Section 4 shows the experimental setup and comprehensive validation of the proposed method. Section 5 concludes this paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temporal prediction for enhancing robustness of physics-informed model

ce:section.ce:para: LSTM networks provide temporal SOC estimation capabilities that complement PPINN's enforcement of physical constraints. While PPINN excels at incorporating governing equations, it requires reliable initial estimates to effectively leverage physics-based relationships. LSTM addresses this need through its sophisticated gating mechanism consisting of forget, input, and output gates that regulate information flow and maintain selective memory over extended sequences. The LSTM framework is particularly valuable for battery SOC estimation because battery state evolution exhibits complex temporal dependencies across multiple time scales. Short-term dependencies include immediate electrochemical responses, while long-term dependencies encompass capacity fade, aging effects, and thermal history impacts. The LSTM's selective memory capability allows it to capture both immediate battery responses and gradual changes, and understand how current SOC depends on historical operating conditions such as charge/discharge patterns and temperature variations [14].

The LSTM network processes batched input features to generate preliminary SOC estimates. The network takes as input a batch of time-series data X ∈
ℝ
B × T × d
, where B is the batch size, T represents the total number of time points, and the d-dimensional feature vector
x t b
=
V t b
I t b
T t b
P t b
CC t b
∈
ℝ d
consists of voltage, current, temperature, power, and cumulative capacity at time t for batch sample b. For each batch sample b = 1, 2, …, B and time point t = 1, 2, …, T, the LSTM performs computations through three gates mechanisms and cell state updates. The structure of the LSTM unit can be observed in Fig. 1 , and the formulas [15] are as follows:
f t b
= σ
W f
h
t − 1
b
x t b

b f

b i

t b
= tanh

+

=

C t b
,
where ⨀ represents element-wise multiplication. The temporal SOC estimation is obtained through a fully connected layer:
(2)
SOC ̂

+

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration of LSTM predictions with the PPINN
ce:section.ce:para:
The integration of LSTM with PPINN creates a synergistic framework that overcomes the individual limitations of data-driven and model-based methods. This synergy is essential for achieving robust and accurate SOC estimation, especially under model uncertainty arising from imprecise physical parameters. The primary role of the LSTM is to provide a temporal SOC trajectory
SOC ̂
that serves as a reliable guide for the PPINN. This temporal SOC trajectory acts as an informed initial condition and an evolving feature input for the PPINN's manifold network. By incorporating this signal, the framework can effectively compensate for inaccuracies in the pre-estimated parameters (
R 0
,
R 1
,
C 1
,
U ocv
) of the equivalent circuit model shown in Fig. 2 . In essence, while the physics-informed component enforces electrochemical laws, the LSTM ensures the solution remains anchored to actual data trends.

Complementing this, the PPINN framework constrains the LSTM's predictions by enforcing physical consistency, ensuring that the final SOC estimation adheres strictly to the fundamental governing equations of battery dynamics. This bidirectional interaction establishes a robust regulatory mechanism that prevents the LSTM component from overfitting to measurement noise or spurious correlations in the training dataset, thereby significantly improving generalization capability, particularly in data-scarce scenarios.

Ultimately, this synergistic guidance overcomes the individual limitations of purely data-driven or model-based methods, leading to SOC estimations that are not only highly accurate but also physically consistent across a wide range of diverse operating conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Overall framework for physics-informed SOC estimation
ce:section.ce:para:
The PINN incorporates physical laws described by a PDE directly into neural network training through automatic differentiation [19]. The general PINN method minimizes the physics residual defined as:
(3)
f ≔ N
u
t x
= 0 , x ∈ Ω , t ∈
0 T
,
where N
∙
represents the differential operator governing the physical system, u
t x
denotes the solution function, x represents spatial coordinates in domain Ω , and t represents time within interval
0 T
. For parameterized systems, the conventional PINN method treats parameters merely as additional coordinates, limiting its effectiveness for diverse parameter conditions. The PPINN framework addresses this limitation by explicitly encoding parameter dependencies into a latent representation, enabling efficient learning across multiple battery operating conditions.

The PPINN learns a continuous, underlying structure known as a latent manifold, which represents the generalized relationship between battery parameters and their dynamic behavior. Instead of treating each operating condition as an isolated problem, a hypernetwork acts as a parameter encoder, translating the SOC-dependent baseline parameter set (
θ base
) into a compressed, abstract representation that serves as a unique coordinate on this manifold. This latent coordinate then dynamically configures the main solution network to generate a physically consistent approximation, denoted as
V ̂
1
t
θ base
. This approximation is designed to be consistent with the governing physical laws by ensuring the parameterized differential operator is minimized, N
t
V ̂
1
t
θ base
≈ 0 . In essence,
θ base
identifies the physical context on the manifold while the LSTM-predicted trajectory (
SOC ̂
) provides evolving data trends, allowing the framework to reflect both generalized physical laws and specific temporal patterns.

To construct this manifold,
θ base
is obtained through one-time offline hybrid pulse power characterization (HPPC) and open circuit voltage (OCV) tests at 0.05 SOC intervals from 0 to 1, resulting in 20 distinct parameter sets, as shown in Fig. 3 . Since this characterization is performed at the battery cell-type level during the cell development phase, all vehicles equipped with the same cell type share identical
θ base
. Consequently, the obtained
θ base
is directly reusable across the entire vehicle fleet without per-vehicle testing once
θ base
is obtained. The baseline parameter set is defined as:
(4)
θ base
=
R 0
SOC
R 1
SOC
C 1
SOC
U ocv
SOC

From these characterization results, the complete OCV-SOC characteristic curve is obtained through polynomial curve fitting of the stabilized voltages at different SOC levels. These parameter sets enable the formulation of 20 corresponding differential equations that represent the battery dynamics across the entire SOC range, providing the physical foundation for the PPINN implementation [32].

Based on these parameter sets, the first-order RC equivalent circuit model shown in Fig. 2 represents the battery dynamics, where the battery parameters—open-circuit voltage
U ocv
SOC
, ohmic resistance
R 0
SOC
, polarization resistance
R 1
SOC
, and polarization capacitance
C 1
SOC
—constitute the elements of
θ base
. The detailed procedures for estimating these parameters are as follows [13].

As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the HPPC test involves discharge and charge current pulses to extract circuit parameters from the battery's voltage response. The ohmic resistance
R 0
is determined from the instantaneous voltage drops at current transitions, calculated for discharge and charge conditions:
(5)
R 0 d
=
V A
−
V B
I
,
R 0 c
=
V E
−
V F
I
,
R 0
=
R 0 d
+
R 0 c
2
.

The polarization parameters are extracted from the exponential voltage recovery segments, where the time constant τ =
R 1
C 1
is identified through curve fitting of the voltage response:
(6)
V t
t
=
U ocv
−
V 1
0
e
−
t τ
(7)
V 1
0
= I ∙
R 1
1 −
e
−
t τ

enabling the determination of both
R 1
and
C 1
from the known time constant and initial polarization voltage. The OCV-SOC relationship is established through a controlled discharge test as shown in Fig. 4(b). During the rest stages (S1, S3), the terminal voltage stabilizes to the equilibrium OCV corresponding to the current SOC, while the load current stage (S2) provides the discharge profile. The voltage drop during current application is characterized by:
(8)
∆
v 0
=
R 0
∙ ∆ i , ∆
v ∞
=
R 0
+
R 1
∙ ∆ i

The equilibrium time constant is approximately 4
R 1
C 1
, ensuring complete relaxation during rest periods.

The overall framework is illustrated in Fig. 5 . The PPINN framework integrates several components to produce physically consistent predictions:
(9)

produces the final solution.

In the proposed PPINN framework as shown in Fig. 5, a coordinate encoder,
g
θ c
, first processes temporal information to generate a hidden representation,
h coord
, capturing temporal evolution. To achieve this, it combines time t with the LSTM-predicted SOC trajectory
SOC ̂
, to form a two-dimensional input,
x coord
, as follows:
(10)
x coord
=
t
SOC ̂
∈
ℝ
B × T × 2

This input is then processed through the coordinate encoder:
(11)
h coord
= σ
W coord
∙
x coord
+
b coord

In this formulation,
W coord
∈
ℝ
h dim
× 2
is the weight matrix,
b coord
∈
ℝ
h dim
is the bias vector, and σ represents the activation function.

To adapt this general concept for the battery SOC estimation problem, the hypernetwork,
g
θ p
, is designed to interpret the SOC-dependent baseline parameter set
θ base
as a unique, parameter-defining input. It then translates this physical context into a dynamic, parameter-specific configuration for the main solution network [26]. This approach enables a single model to effectively learn a generalized solution across a wide range of operating conditions, thereby avoiding the need for repetitive training for each distinct scenario. The input used for the hypernetwork,
μ combined
, is defined as follows:
(12)
μ combined
=
θ base
SOC ̂
: 0 :
∈
ℝ 5
,

with the inputs representing the SOC-dependent baseline parameter set,
θ base
∈
ℝ
N param
× 4
, and the LSTM's initial prediction,
SOC ̂
: 0 :
∈
ℝ
N param
× 1
, respectively. This combined input is passed through the hypernetwork's fully-connected layers to generate dynamic weights. The forward pass begins with an initial embedding layer:
(13)
e 0
=
σ hy
W emb 0
∙
μ combined
+
b emb 0

For intermediate layers, where m ∈
0 1 2 …
M − 1
and M =
n
hy _ layers
− 2 , the computation proceeds as:
(14)

The final layer produces the singular values to be used in the subsequent SVD:
(15)
s
μ combined
=

W emb
M + 1
∙
e M
+

M + 1

In (15), the hypernetwork's final output, s
μ combined
, contains the flattened singular values representing the system's parameter-dependent characteristics. This allows the manifold network to be dynamically modulated to produce a solution adapted to the specific initial conditions [28]. The s
μ combined
is reshaped into a set of singular values
α l
for each
l th
layer of the manifold network:
(16)
α l
= s
μ combined
:
l − 1
∙
σ dim
: l ∙
σ dim
∈
ℝ
σ dim

The SVD approach provides computational efficiency while maintaining representational capacity [29]. Each fully connected layer,
FC l
, within the manifold network is:
(17)
FC l
=
U l
∑ l
α l
V l T

Here,
U l
and
V l
are orthogonal matrices, while
∑ l
α l
= diag
α l
is the diagonal matrix formed from the singular values
α l
generated in (16). This decomposition allows for a highly efficient low-rank matrix multiplication:
(18)
M LR
U l
α l
V l
x
=
U l
∙
α l
⨀
V l
∙ x

This low-rank matrix multiplication requires O
2 ∙
h dim
∙
σ dim
operations, offering a substantial computational saving compared to the O
2 ∙
h dim 2
required for conventional matrix multiplication, particularly when
σ dim
≪
h dim
.

Furthermore, this SVD-based decomposition optimizes model storage requirements, as shown in Fig. 6 . In a conventional approach,
N param
separate full weight matrices
W l
must be stored, one for each distinct operating condition, where
N param
represents the number of parameter sets for
θ base
. As the number of operating conditions increases, the storage demand grows linearly. In contrast, the orthogonal bases
U l
and
V l
can be shared across all
N param
conditions, and only the low-dimensional singular values
α l
need to be dynamically generated by the hypernetwork at inference time, introducing negligible computational overhead. This drastically reduces the storage requirements.

Combined with this storage efficiency, the proposed PPINN follows an offline-training, online-inference paradigm. Once trained, the online inference requires only a single forward pass through the LSTM and the PPINN, with no iterative optimization or fine-tuning required at inference time. This is a significant advantage over model-based methods, which perform recursive matrix operations at every time step.

The manifold network,
g
θ g
, is the core solution-approximating module that integrates the temporal context from the coordinate encoder and the dynamic parameterization from the hypernetwork to produce the final physical prediction. This network solves the governing differential equations of the battery model by mapping the latent coordinate representation
h coord
from (11) to the target output
V ̂
1
t
θ base
. The forward propagation proceeds sequentially across its
D g
layers, where each layer utilizes the computationally efficient SVD-based low-rank matrix multiplication previously defined in (18). In this framework,
h coord
serves as the primary input
x 0
to the network, while the parameters
α l
dynamically modulate the weights of each layer instead of being concatenated as a direct input. The computation for the
l th
hidden layer
h l
is given by:
(19)
h l
=
M LR
U l
α l
V l
x
l − 1
+
b l
where
x
l − 1
is the output from the previous layer, with
x 0
=
h coord
, and
b l
is the bias vector. An activation function σ is then applied to enhance the network's expressiveness:
(20)
x l
= σ
w 0
∙
h l

Following the sequential propagation through all layers, the final output layer produces the predicted RC voltage:
(21)
V ̂
1
t
θ base
=
W out
∙
x
D g
+
b out
.

By dynamically modulating the singular values
α l
based on
θ base
, the manifold network effectively adapts its solution manifold. This integrated framework allows the single neural network to accurately represent the battery's behavior across a wide range of SOC levels and operating conditions without the need for retraining.

Notably, these operating conditions encompass multi-factor variations such as temperature, C-rate, and aging, which are implicitly addressed without requiring explicit factor-specific inputs. Since the input feature vector
x t b
carries the instantaneous operating state, the LSTM encodes the influence of these factors into its prediction
SOC ̂
. This capability is empirically supported by [33], which demonstrated that a single LSTM trained on multi-temperature sensor data generalizes across different ambient temperatures without explicit temperature-specific labeling. Additionally, different driving cycles inherently impose distinct current profiles representing diverse C-rate conditions, and [34] validated successful generalization to untrained aging levels using only voltage, current, and temperature inputs. Integrating this data-driven context with the physics-based
θ base
in
μ combined
enables the hypernetwork to produce dynamic weights that are consistent with physical laws and responsive to environmental shifts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-objective loss function for synergistic learning
ce:section.ce:para:
As illustrated in Fig. 5, the proposed PPINN is trained by minimizing a multi-objective loss function that enforces both data fidelity and physical consistency. This method follows the core principle of PINN, where the network is constrained by the system's governing differential equations. As introduced in (3), the general goal is to minimize the physics residual, defined as f ≔ N
u
. To apply this principle to the battery system, the general differential operator N
∙
is specified using the governing equations derived from the first-order RC equivalent circuit model, shown in Fig. 2. For this model, the electrochemical behavior is described by the differential equation for the RC voltage,
V ̂
1
t
θ base
. Thus, the specific differential operator N
t
V ̂
1
t
θ base
is defined as:
(22)
N
t
V ̂
1
t
θ base
=
d
V ̂
1
t
θ
b ase
dt
+
V ̂
1
t
θ base
R 1
C 1
−
I
t
C 1
.

The fundamental physical law requires this operator to be zero, forming the physics-based residual that the network must learn to minimize. Additionally, the model is constrained by the initial condition, which states that the RC voltage must be zero at the beginning of any operation. These constraints are expressed as:
(23)
N
t
V ̂
1
t
θ base
= 0 ,
(24)
V ̂
1
t = 0
θ base
= 0

The total loss function synergistically combines multiple objectives using an uncertainty-based adaptive weighting technique known as SALB [30], which eliminates the need for manual hyperparameter tuning. The total loss is formulated as:
(25)
L total
=
∑
i ∈
LSTM physics init reg
1 2
exp
−
λ i
L i
+
1 2
λ i
.

The learnable log-variance parameters
λ i
in (25) automatically adjust during training based on the relative magnitudes and uncertainties of each loss component, allowing the optimization to focus on more reliable loss components while maintaining regularization. Eq. (25) is composed of four distinct components that work in synergy, as follows:
(26)
L LSTM
=
1 N
∑
j = 1
N
SOC ̂
j
−
SOC
target , j
2
,
(27)
L physics
=
1 N
∑
j = 1
N
∂
V ̂
1
t
θ base
∂ t
t j
+
V ̂
1
t j
θ base
R 1
C 1
−
I
t j
C 1
2
,
(28)
L init
=
1 N
∑
j = 1
N
V ̂
1
t j
= 0
θ
base , j
2
,
(29)
L reg
=
1 N
∑
j = 1
N
V
pred , j
−
V
measured , j
2
.

Specifically, first, the LSTM loss,
L LSTM
, aligns the model to observed data trends by enforcing consistency between the LSTM's temporal SOC predictions and the ground-truth values. Second, the physics loss,
L physics
, compels the solution to adhere to the fundamental electrochemical laws of the battery model by minimizing the PDE residual from (23). The partial derivative term in the physics loss is computed using automatic differentiation on the full output, which represents the gradient of the network's solution with respect to time:
(30)
∂
V ̂
1
t
θ base
∂ t
=
∂
g
θ g
g
θ c
t
SOC ̂
g
θ p
θ base
SOC ̂
: 0 :
∂ t
.

Third, the initial condition loss,
L init
, ensures the solution begins from a physically valid state at t = 0 by enforcing the boundary constraint from (24). Finally, the voltage regression loss,
L reg
, is a direct empirical data-fitting term, ensuring that the model's overall output accurately matches the measured terminal voltage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test datasets: public and laboratory tested datasets
ce:section.ce:para:
The method proposed in this paper is evaluated on a public dataset for the LG 18650HG2 Li-ion battery, provided by Dr. Philip Kollmeyer at McMaster University [35]. The battery specifications are summarized in Tables 1 and 2
. To ensure reproducibility and precision in the physics-informed learning process, all data were recorded at a consistent sampling frequency of 1 Hz. As summarized in Table 3 , the total public dataset comprises 124,395 samples, providing a sufficiently large-scale dataset to ensure the robustness of neural network training. The experimental protocol encompasses comprehensive battery testing under multiple temperature conditions. Three standardized driving cycles are employed to simulate diverse EV operating scenarios. Specifically, the LA92 cycle represents realistic urban driving patterns with frequent acceleration and deceleration events in metropolitan environments. The US06 cycle evaluates aggressive driving behaviors including high-speed highway conditions and rapid power transitions. The UDDS cycle focuses on stop-and-go city driving characterized by low-speed operations with intermittent idling periods. The current profiles for these public driving cycles are illustrated in Fig. 7 .

To evaluate the model's generalization capability and its adaptability to diverse driving behaviors under various thermal conditions, the training and test datasets were strategically configured. The training dataset comprises UDDS cycles across five temperature conditions (25
°
C , 40
°
C , 0
°
C , −10
°
C , −20
°
C ), while the test dataset consists of LA92 and US06 cycles at the same temperatures. This approach ensures that the model is evaluated on entirely different, unseen dynamic patterns rather than sub-sections of the same cycle, thereby rigorously verifying its generalization capability. To evaluate the method's robustness to missing data, which is relevant for practical applications where continuous high-frequency data collection may not always be feasible, its performance was compared on the entire training dataset against that on a smaller subset created through random sampling. For SOC calculation as label data, the coulomb counting method is employed based on current integration. The SOC at time t is calculated as:
(31)
SOC
t
=
SOC 0
−
1
C n
∫ 0 t
i
τ
dτ ,
where
C n
is the nominal capacity, and i
τ
denotes the current at time τ . The initial SOC value
SOC 0
is determined based on the starting voltage and initial current direction.

To further validate the method's robustness across different battery configurations, independent laboratory experiments were conducted using an INR 21700-33 J lithium-ion battery, providing real-world measured data to verify the framework's performance beyond the public dataset. Detailed specifications for this battery are summarized in Tables 1 and 2. The experimental setup, illustrated in Fig. 8 , features an integrated control and monitoring system. A data acquisition system, composed of Workstation and RTDS, directs the battery testing equipment to apply specified charge/discharge profiles while receiving critical sensing data. To accurately determine the battery's electrochemical characteristics, all experiments were conducted at a constant room temperature of 25
°
C , operating within a voltage range of 2.5 V to 4.2 V and applying a constant charge and discharge current of 2.85 A. To evaluate the model's performance under varying data availability, training data sizes of 10,000, 1000, and 500 samples were examined.

The battery parameters for both the public and laboratory-tested datasets were estimated following the procedures shown in Fig. 4. However, precise identification is fundamentally limited by the nonlinear nature of battery dynamics and environmental sensitivity. While accurate parameter estimation ideally necessitates supplemental experimental validation, the parameters in this paper were derived solely through the methods in Fig. 4, assuming that such additional validation is infeasible in real-world driving scenarios. To ensure a rigorous and fair comparison, these consistently estimated parameters are applied across all methods in the subsequent experimental validation.

Under the same hardware configuration, all simulations were run on a computer using an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2060 SUPER graphics card. The performance evaluation employs root mean square error (RMSE) to quantify the accuracy of SOC estimation. To ensure fair comparison between the proposed and comparative methods, all methods are configured with identical hyperparameters such as hidden layer dimensions, number of layers, maximum iterations and learning rates. Table 4 summarizes the proposed and conventional methods evaluated in this study, along with their methodological categories and descriptions.

The room temperature evaluation provides fundamental validation of the proposed PPINN under controlled standard operating conditions. Testing across LA92 and US06 driving cycles enables direct performance comparison with existing methods. The experimental results and SOC error comparisons at room temperature can be observed in Fig. 9 , while the corresponding RMSE values are presented in Table 5 .

Under the aforementioned parameter estimation constraints, conventional methods demonstrate suboptimal estimation performance. The model-based EKF method requires its process noise covariance Q and measurement noise covariance R to be tuned for each temperature condition and driving cycle due to its inherent model-based characteristics. Despite this condition-specific tuning, the EKF records RMSE values of 4.33% for the LA92 cycle and 5.85% for the US06 cycle, highlighting its sensitivity to the precision of pre-estimated battery parameters. Consequently, the hybrid methods that depend on the EKF exhibit compromised performance.

The parallel EKF-PSO-LSTM joint algorithm [17] and the sequential EKF-LSTM combination algorithm [18] were selected as representative hybrid methods, as shown in Table 4. Specifically, [17] utilizes a parallel fusion framework, in which the EKF and a PSO-LSTM network provide separate estimates that are fused via a Kalman filtering algorithm, while [18] employs a sequential structure in which the EKF's preliminary SOC estimates are used as inputs for an LSTM network for error compensation. These methods were chosen to demonstrate the practical limitations of conventional hybrid strategies when precise parameter identification is not available.

In [17], the RMSEs were 2.23% for LA92 cycle and 3.10% for US06 cycle, while in [18], the errors were 3.05% for LA92 cycle and 4.08% for US06 cycle. This demonstrated that the performance of these hybrid structures is inherently constrained by the accuracy of the underlying EKF, leading to limited estimation accuracy despite their integration of data-driven components. The LSTM demonstrated reasonable performance under a single temperature condition, with RMSE of 1.37% for LA92 cycle and 2.05% for US06 cycle, reflecting its pattern recognition capabilities. Similarly, the PINN showed moderate accuracy, with RMSEs of 1.64% and 1.28% respectively. Although PINN can be accurate for a single dataset, notably on the US06, it is sensitive to the accuracy of parameters and lacks a direct mechanism to mitigate errors from parameter identification.

In contrast, the proposed PPINN achieves superior performance despite using the same simplified parameter estimation, recording RMSE values of 0.83% for LA92 cycle and 1.33% for US06 cycle. This enhanced accuracy stems from its unique framework that integrates governing physical equations directly into the training process via the loss function. Unlike the fixed-parameter hybrid methods in [17,18], which require condition-specific Q and R tuning and remain sensitive to parameter initialization, the hypernetwork component in PPINN generates dynamic, condition-adaptive weights. This allows the model to learn the system's behavior without being rigidly dependent on the accuracy of pre-determined parameters. This synergistic integration of temporal modeling and physics-informed constraints allows the PPINN to overcome the limitations of parameter dependency, providing accurate estimations.

Furthermore, the computational efficiency advantages of the SVD-based low-rank decomposition approach are demonstrated through operational complexity analysis as shown in (18). To systematically investigate the effect of SVD parameters on SOC estimation performance, seven representative configurations spanning from aggressively compressed (
σ dim
= 16) to full-rank (
σ dim
=
h dim
) are compared under room temperature conditions across the LA92 and US06 driving cycles, as summarized in Fig. 11 and Table 6 . The objective is not to identify a globally optimal configuration through exhaustive search, but to efficiently determine a well-suited configuration from representative candidates covering the practically relevant design space. An explicit accuracy-versus-cost comparison is presented in Fig. 12 , where the RMSE is plotted against the number of multiplications for each configuration. Comparing Config 3 with the full-rank Config 4 demonstrates that SVD low-rank decomposition serves as an effective regularizer, filtering out unnecessary noise and enabling the model to focus on the physically meaningful manifold structure. Conversely, excessively aggressive compression (Config 1, Config 2) and configurations with an overly low
σ dim
relative to
h dim
(Config 5, Config 6) yield higher RMSE despite comparable or fewer operations, confirming that Config 3 represents the elbow point—the minimum rank that preserves sufficient representational capacity. Although Config 7 achieves the lowest RMSE of 0.820%, it requires exactly twice the operations of Config 3 for a marginal improvement of only 0.014%, which does not justify the doubled computational burden for resource-constrained embedded systems. Config 3 therefore demonstrates the most favorable accuracy–efficiency trade-off within the evaluated range, and all subsequent experiments are conducted using Config 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SOC estimation at various temperatures and drive cycles
ce:section.ce:para:
Temperature variations significantly impact battery electrochemical behavior through several interconnected mechanisms [36,37]. At low temperatures, increased electrolyte viscosity reduces ionic conductivity, raising
R 0
and altering
R 1
and
τ 1
, resulting in larger voltage drops and slower voltage recovery that directly affect the governing differential equation from (22). At elevated temperatures, accelerated side reactions lead to different parameter shifts. These variations pose a fundamental challenge for fixed-parameter methods, making robust estimation across thermal conditions difficult. Extended evaluation encompasses temperature ranges including 40
°
C , 0
°
C , −10
°
C , and − 20
°
C across both LA92 and US06 driving cycles. The experimental results and SOC error comparisons at various temperatures and driving cycles can be observed in Fig. 13 , while the corresponding RMSE values are presented in Table 7 .

Extreme temperature conditions reveal fundamental weaknesses in conventional and hybrid estimation methods. The LSTM shows severe thermal sensitivity, with its RMSE escalating to 3.80% under the −20
°
C US06 condition. Even with condition-specific Q and R tuning for each temperature and driving cycle, the EKF records RMSE values ranging from 4.17% to 7.84%, showing that its performance remains limited by model accuracy under thermal stress. The hybrid models [17,18] achieve lower RMSE values than the EKF by leveraging their LSTM components, yet still record errors of up to 4.47% and 5.81% respectively, as their accuracy remains partially constrained by the underlying EKF's output quality. The PINN achieves competitive accuracy at room temperature (1.28% on US06), indicating that PINN can be effective when the actual battery behavior closely corresponds to its fixed physical parameters. However, at extreme temperatures, the PINN's performance degrades sharply, with RMSE reaching 3.04%, because the fixed parameters cannot accommodate the shifting battery dynamics such as increased resistance at low temperatures and accelerated side reactions at high temperatures.

In contrast, the PPINN maintains exceptional thermal robustness, with RMSE values consistently constrained between 0.98% and 1.59% across all temperature and driving combinations. This robustness is achieved through two complementary mechanisms. First, the physics loss constrains the solution space to physically plausible trajectories, while the data-driven loss provides temperature-dependent corrections. The SALB from (25) dynamically balances these objectives when the physics loss becomes unreliable due to temperature-induced parameter deviations. The learnable
λ i
parameter automatically reduces the relative weight assigned to the unreliable component. Second, for the same
θ base
, the hypernetwork receives different
SOC ̂
depending on the temperature condition, because the LSTM produces temperature-aware predictions from the input feature vector
x t b
. This means the hypernetwork implicitly generates different
α l
for different temperatures, enabling the manifold network to produce temperature-adapted solutions. This adaptability eliminates the need for temperature-specific retraining or parameter re-estimation, confirming the method's superior generalization capability for practical BMS.

To evaluate the proposed method's robustness to missing data in the training sequence, two complementary evaluations are conducted: (1) missing data induced by random sampling, and (2) missing data induced by contiguous segment removal. The two strategies are shown in Fig. 14 .

As shown in Fig. 14 (a), 5000 samples per temperature condition are randomly selected from the original UDDS cycles (Table 3) and re-sorted in chronological order before training. Although the selected samples preserve their chronological order, the resulting training sequence contains numerous irregular temporal gaps where consecutive observations are missing. This disrupts the temporal continuity that LSTM-based models rely on to capture sequential dependencies. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 15 , while Table 8 quantifies the performance metrics.

As demonstrated in Table 8, the LSTM's performance degrades significantly under this condition (reaching RMSE of 3.94% at −20 °C), because the LSTM's hidden state propagation becomes unreliable when the temporal context between consecutive inputs is broken. The PINN also exhibits a significant performance drop, with RMSE values reaching up to 3.72%, as the reduced number of training points limits the data-driven correction needed to compensate for its fixed parameter inaccuracies. In contrast, the proposed PPINN maintains high accuracy (RMSE between 0.92% and 1.71%) across all temperature conditions. This is because the physics loss enforces the governing electrochemical equations at every available training point, regardless of temporal gaps, thereby providing continuous physical constraints that guide the learning process even when a significant portion of temporal observations is unavailable. It is observed that the hybrid models [17,18] generally show degraded performance with the reduced training data, as their LSTM components—which rely on temporal continuity for sequential pattern learning—are directly affected by the irregular temporal gaps introduced through random sampling. Since [17] fuses the LSTM's estimation with the EKF's output and [18] uses the EKF's preliminary estimates as inputs for LSTM-based error compensation, the degraded LSTM performance propagates through the entire hybrid pipeline. For example, the RMSE of [17] increases from 2.93% (Table 7) to 3.85% at 40 °C US06, and [18] increases from 1.97% (Table 7) to 2.30% at 40 °C LA92. However, in certain conditions, slight performance improvements are observed compared to the full dataset—for instance, [17] decreases from 2.60% (Table 7) to 2.47% at 0 °C LA92, and [18] decreases from 4.08% (Table 5) to 3.85% at 25 °C US06. This can be attributed to a reduced influence of the LSTM component, which prevents overfitting to noise present in the larger dataset, allowing the EKF's physical model to provide a more stable basis. Nevertheless, this improvement is inconsistent across conditions, underscoring the fundamental instability of hybrid methods that depend on both temporal data quality and model-based accuracy simultaneously.

To further verify the PPINN's robustness, additional experiments are conducted where contiguous segments are removed from the training set at predefined intervals [38,39], as shown in Fig. 14 (b). This method creates continuous gaps that pose a more severe challenge to temporal learning. Since this evaluation aims to verify the contribution of the physics-informed constraints under temporal discontinuity, the proposed PPINN is compared against the LSTM as the purely data-driven baseline. Table 9 presents the specific intervals over which training data is removed for each temperature condition, and Table 10 summarizes the corresponding RMSE values.

The LSTM suffers severe performance degradation under these conditions, with RMSE values reaching up to 11.06% (40 °C, US06), because the continuous absence of data prevents the LSTM from maintaining meaningful hidden state representations across the missing intervals. The proposed PPINN also exhibits higher RMSE values (2.60%–5.68%) compared to the random sampling scenarios, despite the remaining training data being larger in volume than the 5000 samples per temperature condition used in the random sampling scenarios. This confirms that although the reduced data volume contributes to the performance degradation, the disrupted temporal continuity has a more significant impact, which directly affects the PPINN's own LSTM component. Nevertheless, the PPINN consistently outperforms the LSTM by a significant margin across all conditions. This is because the physics loss provides an independent source of supervision that does not depend on temporal continuity—it evaluates compliance with the governing electrochemical equations at each individual training point, thereby preserving the solution's physical consistency even across the missing segments.

To further validate the proposed method's performance, experimental datasets collected from laboratory battery testing are employed. Unlike previous sections where different datasets were used for training and testing, this section evaluates performance on the learned data distribution by partitioning the same experimental dataset into training and testing sets at an 8:2 ratio. This approach enables validation of the method's capability to learn and accurately predict within the same data domain. The experimental validation utilizes OCV test datasets as described in Table 2. Fig. 17 presents the SOC estimation results and SOC errors for three distinct training data sizes of 10,000, 1000, and 500 samples to evaluate performance scalability across different data availability scenarios. Table 11 shows the corresponding performance metrics.

Fig. 18 shows the training loss curves for the laboratory-tested datasets across three training data sizes. As the training data decreases from 10,000 to 500 samples, the LSTM exhibits increasingly unstable loss trajectories due to its sole reliance on temporal data patterns. In contrast, the PPINN maintains relatively stable convergence by leveraging the physics-informed constraints as a continuous regularizer that compensates for the reduced data availability.

Notably, in Fig. 18 (b) and (c), periodic spikes are observed in the PPINN's loss curve, which are attributed to the SALB mechanism dynamically re-weighting
L LSTM
and
L physics
when their relative magnitudes shift during training. In contrast, Fig. 18 (a) exhibits smooth convergence, as the two loss components maintain a more consistent balance throughout the optimization, requiring less frequent SALB re-adjustment.

Experimental results demonstrate that when trained with a sufficient dataset of 10,000 samples, both the PPINN and the LSTM achieve high accuracy, with RMSE values of 0.40% and 0.66%, respectively. This indicates that both methods possess adequate learning capacity given sufficient data. In contrast, the EKF exhibits a significantly higher RMSE of 6.73%, highlighting its inherent limitations related to the precision of the predefined model parameters. With only 1000 training samples, the LSTM's RMSE degraded to 2.58%, whereas the PPINN maintained a robust performance of 1.37%. This trend is more pronounced with a severely constrained dataset of 500 samples, where the LSTM's RMSE increased to 32.88%, demonstrating its inability to extract meaningful patterns from scarce data.

Under this data-scarce condition, the EKF, with an RMSE of 6.59%, yields a more stable estimation than the LSTM. The EKF's performance is contingent on its underlying physical model rather than the number of training samples, which accounts for its relative stability. However, the proposed PPINN still achieves superior performance with an RMSE of 2.51%, delivering higher accuracy than both the EKF and the LSTM. The overshooting observed in PPINN, as shown in Fig. 17, is a characteristic of the physics-informed approach, particularly with limited data. This occurs when the physics-based loss function attempts to approximate the sudden changes in the current profile, as illustrated in Fig. 8, a transient effect that diminishes as more training data becomes available. These results confirm the proposed method's ability to leverage physical laws to compensate for data scarcity. The physics-informed constraints provide essential electrochemical knowledge, preventing the severe performance degradation observed in the LSTM and enabling effective learning from minimal experimental data. This confirms the method's practical applicability in real-world scenarios where extensive data collection can be constrained by time and resources.

This paper presented a novel PPINN method that synergistically integrates temporal pattern recognition with physics-informed constraints for SOC estimation. The proposed method effectively overcomes the fundamental limitations of data dependency in purely data-driven methods and parameter sensitivity in model-based methods. The primary technical advantage of this method, which accounts for its superior estimation accuracy, is the hypernetwork framework that generates dynamic, condition-adaptive weights. Unlike conventional hybrid structures that rely on fixed parameters, this innovation enables a single model to adaptively learn across multiple operating scenarios without relying on the precision of parameter pre-estimation or frequent retraining. The simultaneous learning of data-driven patterns and electrochemical principles, enforced through a multi-objective loss function, ensures both physical consistency and high fidelity. Quantitative experimental validation confirmed the method's high precision and robustness across diverse conditions. The proposed PPINN achieved exceptional accuracy with RMSE values ranging from 0.83% to 1.59% across a wide thermal range. Furthermore, the method demonstrated robustness to missing data by maintaining reliable performance even with reduced training samples, with RMSE values remaining below 1.71% under random sampling-based data reduction for the public dataset. To further verify its practical applicability, independent laboratory experiments were conducted, achieving an RMSE as low as 0.4% and remaining below 2.51% even under extreme data-scarce conditions, which underscores the method's capability for high-fidelity estimation under limited data availability. These characteristics, combined with the efficient model storage through shared SVD bases, one-time offline parameter acquisition at the cell-type level, and the hypernetwork's dynamic adaptability to varying operating conditions, render the proposed PPINN a practical and reliable solution for real-time BMS deployment.

Yu Seok Jang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Youngjin Kim: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Jonghoon Kim: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. RS-2023-00218377).

in Proc. IEEE Vehicle Power and Propulsion Conf. (VPPC)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Online estimations of li-ion battery SOC and SOH applicable to partial charge/discharge
IEEE Trans. Transp. Electrif.
